packs.name: Flipt
packs.description: Protects against destructive Flipt CLI and API operations.

structural_patterns.name: flipt-flag-delete
structural_patterns.reason: flipt flag delete permanently removes a feature flag. This cannot be undone.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a Flipt feature flag immediately removes it from evaluation. All applications checking this flag will receive default values or errors. Rules, variants, and distributions associated with the flag are also removed.

Safer alternatives:
- flipt flag get: Review flag configuration first
- Disable the flag instead of deleting
- Export flag configuration before deletion

structural_patterns.name: flipt-segment-delete
structural_patterns.reason: flipt segment delete removes a segment and its constraints.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a segment removes user grouping rules. Any flags using this segment for targeting will lose that targeting logic, potentially changing which users receive which variants.

Safer alternatives:
- flipt segment get: Review segment configuration
- Check which flags reference this segment
- Update flags to use different segments first

structural_patterns.name: flipt-namespace-delete
structural_patterns.reason: flipt namespace delete removes a namespace and all its flags, segments, and rules.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a namespace removes ALL flags, segments, rules, and constraints within it. This is a complete wipe of that feature flag environment. All applications using this namespace will lose flag evaluations.

Safer alternatives:
- flipt namespace get: Review namespace contents
- Export all flags and segments before deletion
- Migrate resources to another namespace first

structural_patterns.name: flipt-rule-delete
structural_patterns.reason: flipt rule delete removes a targeting rule from a flag.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a rule changes how the flag evaluates for targeted users. Users previously matching this rule will fall through to other rules or receive the default variant.

Safer alternatives:
- flipt rule get: Review rule configuration
- Disable the rule instead of deleting
- Verify other rules handle affected users

structural_patterns.name: flipt-constraint-delete
structural_patterns.reason: flipt constraint delete removes a constraint from a segment.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Removing a constraint changes segment membership. Users previously excluded or included by this constraint will now match or not match the segment differently.

Safer alternatives:
- Review segment membership before and after
- Test constraint changes in a staging namespace
- Document why the constraint is being removed

structural_patterns.name: flipt-variant-delete
structural_patterns.reason: flipt variant delete removes a variant from a flag.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a variant removes a possible flag value. Rules distributing traffic to this variant will fail or need adjustment. Applications expecting this variant value may break.

Safer alternatives:
- Check which rules reference this variant
- Update distributions before deleting
- Zero out traffic to this variant first

structural_patterns.name: flipt-distribution-delete
structural_patterns.reason: flipt distribution delete removes a distribution from a rule.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Removing a distribution changes traffic allocation within a rule. The affected variant will no longer receive traffic from this rule, shifting all traffic to remaining distributions.

Safer alternatives:
- Review current traffic allocation
- Adjust percentages instead of deleting
- Verify other distributions handle the traffic

structural_patterns.name: flipt-api-delete
structural_patterns.reason: DELETE request to Flipt API can remove flags, segments, or rules.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Direct API DELETE calls to Flipt can remove flags, segments, rules, or namespaces without CLI confirmation prompts. API deletions take effect immediately across all connected applications.

Safer alternatives:
- Use the Flipt CLI for better confirmation prompts
- GET the resource first to verify what will be deleted
- Use the Flipt UI for visibility into dependencies

packs.name: LaunchDarkly
packs.description: Protects against destructive LaunchDarkly CLI and API operations.

structural_patterns.name: ldcli-flags-delete
structural_patterns.reason: ldcli flags delete permanently removes a feature flag. This cannot be undone.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a LaunchDarkly flag permanently removes it from all environments. SDKs will return default values, potentially breaking feature gates. Targeting rules, prerequisites, and experiment data are lost.

Safer alternatives:
- ldcli flags archive: Soft-delete with recovery option
- Turn off the flag in all environments first
- Export flag configuration before deletion

structural_patterns.name: ldcli-flags-archive
structural_patterns.reason: ldcli flags archive soft-deletes a feature flag. While recoverable, this affects all environments.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Archiving a flag removes it from the flag list and evaluation. While archived flags can be restored, SDKs will stop receiving the flag and return defaults immediately across all environments.

Safer alternatives:
- Turn off the flag before archiving
- Verify no code paths depend on the flag
- Document the flag's purpose before archiving

structural_patterns.name: ldcli-projects-delete
structural_patterns.reason: ldcli projects delete removes an entire project and all its flags, environments, and settings.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a project removes ALL flags, environments, segments, and metrics within it. This is irreversible and affects all applications using this project's SDK keys.

Safer alternatives:
- Export project configuration first
- Archive flags individually to preserve recovery options
- Migrate critical flags to another project

structural_patterns.name: ldcli-environments-delete
structural_patterns.reason: ldcli environments delete removes an environment and all its flag configurations.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting an environment removes all flag configurations, targeting rules, and SDK keys for that environment. Applications using this environment's SDK key will fail to connect.

Safer alternatives:
- Rotate SDK keys before deletion
- Export environment configuration
- Turn off all flags in the environment first

structural_patterns.name: ldcli-segments-delete
structural_patterns.reason: ldcli segments delete removes a user segment and its targeting rules.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a segment removes user grouping rules across all environments. Flags targeting this segment will lose that targeting, changing which users receive which variations.

Safer alternatives:
- Review which flags use this segment
- Update flag targeting before deletion
- Export segment configuration

structural_patterns.name: ldcli-metrics-delete
structural_patterns.reason: ldcli metrics delete removes a metric and its experiment data.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a metric removes the metric definition and all associated experiment results. Historical experiment data for this metric is lost and cannot be recovered.

Safer alternatives:
- Export experiment results first
- Archive the metric if possible
- Document metric findings before deletion

structural_patterns.name: launchdarkly-api-delete-environments
structural_patterns.reason: DELETE request to LaunchDarkly API removes environments.
structural_patterns.explanation:
API DELETE calls to environments immediately invalidate SDK keys and remove all flag configurations for that environment. Connected applications will lose flag evaluations.

Safer alternatives:
- Use ldcli for better confirmation prompts
- GET the environment config first
- Rotate SDK keys before deletion

structural_patterns.name: launchdarkly-api-delete-flags
structural_patterns.reason: DELETE request to LaunchDarkly API removes feature flags.
structural_patterns.explanation:
API DELETE calls permanently remove flags without archive recovery options. All targeting rules, variations, and prerequisites are lost across all environments.

Safer alternatives:
- Use ldcli flags archive for soft-delete
- GET the flag configuration first
- Use the LaunchDarkly UI for visibility

structural_patterns.name: launchdarkly-api-delete-segments
structural_patterns.reason: DELETE request to LaunchDarkly API removes segments.
structural_patterns.explanation:
API DELETE calls to segments remove user groupings immediately. Flags using this segment will lose targeting rules, changing feature behavior for those users.

Safer alternatives:
- Check segment dependencies first
- Update flag targeting before deletion
- Export segment membership

structural_patterns.name: launchdarkly-api-delete-projects
structural_patterns.reason: DELETE request to LaunchDarkly API removes projects.
structural_patterns.explanation:
API DELETE calls to projects remove ALL resources: flags, environments, segments, metrics, and SDK keys. This is the most destructive operation and cannot be undone.

Safer alternatives:
- Export project configuration completely
- Use ldcli for confirmation prompts
- Contact LaunchDarkly support for assistance

structural_patterns.name: launchdarkly-api-delete-generic
structural_patterns.reason: DELETE request to LaunchDarkly API can remove resources.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Generic DELETE requests to the LaunchDarkly API can remove various resources including webhooks, integrations, teams, and access tokens. Review the specific endpoint before executing.

Safer alternatives:
- Verify the exact resource being deleted
- Use the LaunchDarkly UI for better visibility
- GET the resource first to confirm

packs.name: Split.io
packs.description: Protects against destructive Split.io CLI and API operations.

structural_patterns.name: split-splits-delete
structural_patterns.reason: split splits delete permanently removes a split definition. This cannot be undone.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a split permanently removes the feature flag definition and all its targeting rules. SDKs will return control treatment for this split. Historical data and metrics are preserved but the split cannot be recovered.

Safer alternatives:
- split splits kill: Stop traffic without deleting
- Archive the split in the UI
- Export split configuration first

structural_patterns.name: split-splits-kill
structural_patterns.reason: split splits kill terminates a split, stopping all traffic to treatments.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Killing a split immediately stops all treatment assignment and returns the default treatment to all users. Unlike delete, the split can be reactivated, but all users will see behavior change immediately.

Safer alternatives:
- Gradually ramp down traffic percentages first
- Verify the default treatment behavior
- Communicate the change to stakeholders

structural_patterns.name: split-environments-delete
structural_patterns.reason: split environments delete removes an environment and all its configurations.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting an environment removes all split configurations, targeting rules, and API keys for that environment. Applications using this environment will receive default treatments for all splits.

Safer alternatives:
- Export environment configuration
- Rotate API keys before deletion
- Kill all splits in the environment first

structural_patterns.name: split-segments-delete
structural_patterns.reason: split segments delete removes a segment and its targeting rules.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a segment removes user grouping definitions. Splits targeting this segment will lose that targeting rule, changing which users receive which treatments.

Safer alternatives:
- Check which splits use this segment
- Update split targeting before deletion
- Export segment membership

structural_patterns.name: split-traffic-types-delete
structural_patterns.reason: split traffic-types delete removes a traffic type. This affects all splits using it.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a traffic type affects ALL splits configured for that traffic type. SDKs sending this traffic type will no longer match any splits, returning control treatment for all evaluations.

Safer alternatives:
- Review all splits using this traffic type
- Migrate splits to a different traffic type
- Ensure no SDKs are sending this traffic type

structural_patterns.name: split-workspaces-delete
structural_patterns.reason: split workspaces delete removes a workspace and all its resources.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a workspace removes ALL splits, segments, environments, and API keys within it. This is the most destructive operation and affects all applications using any resource in this workspace.

Safer alternatives:
- Export complete workspace configuration
- Migrate critical splits to another workspace
- Contact Split.io support for assistance

structural_patterns.name: split-api-delete-splits
structural_patterns.reason: DELETE request to Split.io API removes split definitions.
structural_patterns.explanation:
API DELETE calls to splits permanently remove feature flags without CLI confirmation. All targeting rules and treatments are lost immediately.

Safer alternatives:
- Use the Split CLI for confirmation prompts
- GET the split configuration first
- Use the Split UI for visibility into impact

structural_patterns.name: split-api-delete-environments
structural_patterns.reason: DELETE request to Split.io API removes environments.
structural_patterns.explanation:
API DELETE calls to environments invalidate all API keys and remove all split configurations for that environment. Applications will lose all feature flag evaluations.

Safer alternatives:
- Use the Split CLI for better confirmation
- Export environment configuration first
- Rotate API keys before deletion

structural_patterns.name: split-api-delete-segments
structural_patterns.reason: DELETE request to Split.io API removes segments.
structural_patterns.explanation:
API DELETE calls to segments remove user groupings. Splits using this segment will lose targeting rules, changing treatment assignment for affected users.

Safer alternatives:
- Check segment dependencies first
- Update split targeting before deletion
- Export segment membership data

structural_patterns.name: split-api-delete-generic
structural_patterns.reason: DELETE request to Split.io API can remove resources.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Generic DELETE requests to the Split.io API can remove various resources. Review the specific endpoint to understand what will be deleted.

Safer alternatives:
- Verify the exact resource being deleted
- Use the Split CLI or UI for better visibility
- GET the resource first to confirm

packs.name: Unleash
packs.description: Protects against destructive Unleash CLI and API operations.

structural_patterns.name: unleash-features-delete
structural_patterns.reason: unleash features delete permanently removes a feature toggle. This cannot be undone.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting an Unleash feature toggle permanently removes it and all its strategies. SDKs will return the default value (typically false). Event history is preserved but the toggle cannot be recovered.

Safer alternatives:
- unleash features archive: Soft-delete with recovery
- Disable the toggle in all environments first
- Export toggle configuration before deletion

structural_patterns.name: unleash-features-archive
structural_patterns.reason: unleash features archive soft-deletes a feature toggle.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Archiving a toggle removes it from evaluation but allows recovery. SDKs will stop receiving the toggle and return default values. Archived toggles can be restored from the Unleash UI.

Safer alternatives:
- Disable the toggle before archiving
- Verify no code paths depend on the toggle
- Document the toggle's purpose before archiving

structural_patterns.name: unleash-projects-delete
structural_patterns.reason: unleash projects delete removes a project and all its feature toggles.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a project removes ALL feature toggles, strategies, and environments within it. This is irreversible. All applications using toggles from this project will receive default values.

Safer alternatives:
- Export project configuration first
- Archive toggles individually for recovery options
- Migrate critical toggles to another project

structural_patterns.name: unleash-environments-delete
structural_patterns.reason: unleash environments delete removes an environment.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting an environment removes all toggle configurations for that environment. API keys for this environment stop working. Applications will fail to fetch toggle states.

Safer alternatives:
- Export environment configuration
- Disable toggles in the environment first
- Rotate API keys before deletion

structural_patterns.name: unleash-strategies-delete
structural_patterns.reason: unleash strategies delete removes a custom strategy.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a custom strategy breaks all toggles using it. Those toggles will fail to evaluate properly, potentially returning unexpected values or errors.

Safer alternatives:
- Check which toggles use this strategy
- Migrate toggles to built-in strategies first
- Create a replacement strategy before deleting

structural_patterns.name: unleash-api-keys-delete
structural_patterns.reason: unleash api-keys delete removes an API key.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting an API key immediately revokes access for all SDKs using that key. Applications will fail to connect and receive toggle updates, falling back to cached or default values.

Safer alternatives:
- Create new API keys before deleting old ones
- Update SDK configurations first
- Use key rotation instead of deletion

structural_patterns.name: unleash-api-delete-features
structural_patterns.reason: DELETE request to Unleash API removes feature toggles.
structural_patterns.explanation:
API DELETE calls to features permanently remove toggles without archive recovery options. All strategies and configurations are lost immediately.

Safer alternatives:
- Use the Unleash CLI for confirmation prompts
- Archive toggles instead of deleting
- GET the toggle configuration first

structural_patterns.name: unleash-api-delete-projects
structural_patterns.reason: DELETE request to Unleash API removes projects.
structural_patterns.explanation:
API DELETE calls to projects remove ALL toggles, strategies, and configurations within the project. This is the most destructive operation and cannot be undone.

Safer alternatives:
- Export project configuration completely
- Use the Unleash UI for visibility
- Archive toggles individually first

structural_patterns.name: unleash-api-delete-generic
structural_patterns.reason: DELETE request to Unleash API can remove resources.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Generic DELETE requests to the Unleash admin API can remove various resources including strategies, environments, users, and API keys. Review the specific endpoint before executing.

Safer alternatives:
- Verify the exact resource being deleted
- Use the Unleash CLI or UI for better visibility
- GET the resource first to confirm
